title: Declassified cable reinforces proliferation concerns about high-assay low-enriched uranium fuel - Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists
text:
By Edwin Lyman | Analysis | November 7, 2025
A recently declassified document from nearly 50 years ago provides an important piece of the puzzle for open-source researchers seeking to understand the murky origins of the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) system for safeguarding against the diversion of civil nuclear materials for weapons. The document also reinforces concerns about the proliferation potential of small modular reactors that require fuels using uranium enriched from 10 to less than 20 percent uranium 235—that is, fuels that contain the material known as high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU).[1]
HALEU is a subcategory of low-enriched uranium (LEU), which is uranium enriched to below 20 percent uranium 235, and the IAEA has long considered LEU, including HALEU, to be “indirect-use material.” For the agency, HALEU cannot be used to make a nuclear weapon without converting it to highly enriched uranium (HEU) by further enriching it to 20 percent or above—a significant technical barrier for all but a few countries. Consequently, HALEU is subject to far less stringent international safeguards than HEU.
But the newly uncovered document reveals that, in 1977, the US government recommended to the IAEA that, contrary to its previous position, the agency should consider enriched uranium in the HALEU range to be a material “of direct utility in an … explosive device.” That is, the United States advised that HALEU should be treated similarly to HEU and be subject to stricter safeguards—a recommendation that the IAEA apparently rejected. But given the current international push for rapid deployment of reactors that will need large quantities of HALEU fuel, it is time for the IAEA to reconsider that decision.
Proliferation risk of HALEU fuel. The Energy Department, with bipartisan support from Congress, is now vigorously promoting the global deployment of “advanced” nuclear power reactors that require HALEU-based fuels, as well as the facilities needed to enrich and fabricate those fuels. For example, nearly all of the 11 reactor designs selected by the Energy Department for its New Reactor Pilot Program will use HALEU fuel. And Russia, which has already deployed two barge-mounted small modular reactors (SMRs) using HALEU fuel, is planning to deploy others in Uzbekistan and elsewhere around the globe.
But without appropriate constraints, large-scale production and use of HALEU may greatly increase the risks of nuclear proliferation and terrorism.
The light-water-cooled nuclear power reactors (also known as LWRs) that comprise most of the world’s operating fleet use LEU containing around 5 percent uranium 235. This fuel is considered unattractive for nation-states or terrorists seeking nuclear weapons because it is not directly usable in a bomb. To produce HEU from 5-percent-enriched fuel, proliferators would need access to a uranium enrichment capability, which is a highly controlled, complex, and expensive technology available to only a few countries. And although HALEU appears somewhat more attractive than LEU at lower enrichments because it would require less separative work (enrichment effort) to be converted to HEU, it is still treated as a material of low proliferation concern.
However, earlier this year, the late Richard Garwin and I—along with professors Scott Kemp of MIT, Mark Deinert of the Colorado School of Mines, and Frank von Hippel of Princeton— presented evidence in a letter to Science that HALEU may be used to make nuclear weapons without the need to enrich it further, and we called for further study of the issue by the US government. The concern is that a state or a terrorist group that illicitly obtained enough HALEU—typically, one reactor core’s worth or less, depending on the design—could have a far easier path to acquiring a bomb than if it only had access to conventional LWR fuel.
This is a troubling finding because it implies that current international nonproliferation standards for safeguarding HALEU do not provide a level of assurance commensurate with its actual weapon-usability. If further analysis confirms this gap in protection, then those standards need to be urgently strengthened, or else the increasing amount of HALEU in commercial use will pose an unacceptable threat.
The National Nuclear Security Administration (NNSA), the Energy Department’s semi-autonomous agency whose mission includes preventing the spread of nuclear weapons around the world, has publicly acknowledged that expanded use of HALEU may pose proliferation concerns, without confirming that it is directly weapon-usable. Responding to our Science article, former NNSA Administrator Jill Hruby wrote in January that “it is important to address proliferation concerns about HALEU and important to responsibly develop A/SMRs [advanced and small modular reactors].” Hruby added that the NNSA was finalizing plans to commission a National Academies report on the issue.
But there is no public indication that the NNSA under President Trump is heeding Hruby’s warning and restraining the promotion of a HALEU fuel cycle by the Energy Department’s Office of Nuclear Energy, the nuclear power development side of the house. And soon after the change in administration, the NNSA suspended the planned National Academies HALEU study, although there is hope that work will resume now that a new NNSA administrator, Brandon Williams, has finally been confirmed. In the meantime, however, the Energy Department continues to move forward with many HALEU-related projects.
The technical basis for the IAEA’s original categorization of HALEU as indirect-use material is not clear from open-source information. It dates back to at least 1976, with the first IAEA Safeguards Technical Manual pointing out that, as uranium enrichment decreases below about 20 percent, the fast critical mass (a surrogate for the minimum amount of material needed to make a bomb) begins to rapidly increase. Based on this observation, the consensus was that isotopic enrichment or irradiation of uranium below 20 percent “is considered to be required to produce material from which nuclear explosive devices could be manufactured.” But that conclusion was not a definitive statement on weapon usability, as there are ways to mitigate the penalties associated with weapons of larger critical mass.
What the declassified cable does—and doesn’t—say. In 1977, the IAEA Standing Advisory Group on Safeguards Implementation submitted recommendations for the quantitative criteria that the agency should use in evaluating the effectiveness of safeguards, which had not been clearly defined at that time. The recommendations of the advisory board, composed of individuals drawn from the United States and other member states, included proposed values for “quantities of safeguards significance” of various nuclear materials. But the reports from this advisory board are confidential, so the public cannot know what the committee recommended at the time.
The IAEA’s first safeguards glossary, published in 1980, defined high-enriched uranium (enrichment 20 percent or more) as direct-use material, with a “significant quantity” (commonly understood as the approximate amount of material that could be used to build one Nagasaki-like nuclear weapon) of 25 kilograms of contained uranium 235. It also defined low-enriched uranium (enrichment below 20 percent) as indirect-use material, with a significant quantity of 75 kilograms of contained uranium 235. These values remain the basis for the agency’s inspection goals today.
However, the October 1977 cable of the US State Department recently declassified and disclosed by the US Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) reveals something unexpected: The United States recommended that the IAEA establish two new categories—“high fissile content uranium” and “low fissile content uranium”—with a dividing line at 10 percent enrichment, in contrast to the 20 percent dividing line between HEU and LEU.
According to the cable, high fissile content uranium would be a quantity “of direct utility in an explosive device,” whereas low fissile content uranium would be of “indirect utility … [and] require further processing.” The document goes on to say that “we would be prepared to accept categorization based on 20 percent uranium 235 but understand that the IAEA has practical problems with making distinctions at the 20 percent value.” (There is no further explanation of what those “practical problems” were in the document.) It also says that “we do not support an intermediate category for 10 to 25 percent uranium 235.”
The document reveals that the United States apparently sought to lower the enrichment threshold that the IAEA had formerly used to define direct-use enriched uranium from 20 percent to 10 percent—thereby including the enrichment range now known as HALEU. To my knowledge, this information was not previously known to the public, and a cursory web search does not turn up any other mention of the new terms proposed in the cable.
The cable was attached to an NRC staff document from February 1978 that complained that the State Department had ignored NRC concerns and issued the cable without its assent. But the State Department apparently had the approval of the other members of an interagency steering group, including the Energy Department and the Arms Control and Disarmament Agency, then independent of State.
One of the NRC’s problems with the cable was that the proposal for the new categories of enriched uranium was not consistent with the United States’s own classification, which used 20 percent as a dividing line between direct- and indirect-use uranium. The NRC asserted that the new criteria were based primarily on input from the Arms Control and Disarmament Agency, but that it had provided “no rationale” for the change. However, the proposal presumably did have a technical basis, as the other agencies were certainly privy to the most sensitive information about nuclear weapon design available—information that may not have been shared with the NRC at the time.
It is known from the now-declassified 1954 “Hafstad” memorandum—named after the former US Atomic Energy Commission official who authored the document[2]—that the commission was aware that uranium enriched in the HALEU range could be used directly to make weapons, although the practicality of doing so was the subject of debate. It is possible that, based on a highly classified understanding of HALEU’s weapon usability, other agencies concluded that international standards for safeguarding HALEU should be more stringent than US domestic standards.
Need for an international review. The backstory of why the IAEA ultimately rejected the US recommendation to its advisory group on safeguards for a 10-percent lower bound on direct-use enriched uranium remains a mystery. Another recently declassified NRC document from the same period refers to an April 1978 meeting of IAEA member states during which some participants “voiced serious concern” over the provisional values for significant quantities and detection timelines that the IAEA had adopted and points out that “considerable effort is still required to establish acceptable criteria for verification.”
One can only surmise that there was significant pushback from other member states against expanding the category of uranium requiring the most stringent (and costly) verification measures. The fact that the cable said the United States was “prepared to accept” a 20-percent threshold means that it was probably not going to fall on its sword over the issue.
The NRC’s objections to tightening nonproliferation controls on HALEU may have ultimately won out. But the cable strongly suggests that other US government agencies were concerned enough about the weapon usability of enriched uranium in the HALEU range to challenge the status quo and recommend that it be safeguarded as intensely as HEU. Such concerns should be even more salient today. An international review of HALEU’s proliferation risks is urgently needed before any more power reactors running on HALEU fuel are deployed.
Notes
[1] “HALEU” was not a term in use in the 1970s. However, for convenience, I use it here generally to describe low-enriched uranium with a uranium 235 content from 10 to just below 20 percent, the threshold for highly enriched uranium. (Uranium enriched between 5 and 10 percent is often called “LEU+”.). The “high-assay” in HALEU simply means it is more highly enriched than conventional light-water-cooled reactor fuel.
[2] The memorandum aimed to define the type and maximum quantity of enriched uranium the United States could export under the Atoms for Peace program without creating undue proliferation risk.

Keywords: HALEU, IAEA, IAEA safeguards, Jill Hruby, LWRs, NNSA, NRC, Richard Garwin, SMRs, US State Department, advanced modular reactors, declassified document, enriched uranium, highly enriched uranium, low-enriched uranium, nuclear proliferation, nuclear reactors
Topics: Nuclear Energy, Nuclear Weapons
The IAEA safeguards problem is less than meets the eye. While the IAEA does define “direct use material” and “significant quantity” as stated, those definitions are less directly tied to safeguards activities under what the IAEA calls the State Level Concept (SLC). Under the SLC, the IAEA uses an analytical method called acquisition pathway analysis to estimate how long it would take for a state to acquire enough weapon-usable nuclear material for a nuclear explosive and, based on that, what safeguards measures it needs to detect that acquisition before it can happen. Bottom line: The IAEA should pay greater attention… Read more »
The IAEA’s “Significant Quantity:” What It Is and What It Isn’t George M. Moore Scientist in Residence Middlebury Institute of International Studies at Monterey In a recent excellent article in the Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists, author Ed Lyman of the Union of Concerned Scientists considered the proliferation potential of uranium below the 20% breakpoint between Highly Enriched Uranium and High Assay Low Enriched Uranium (HALEU) material. He referred to the IAEA “Significant Quantity” (SQ) of special nuclear material (special fissile material in IAEA parlance) as follows: The IAEA’s first safeguards glossary, published in 1980, defined high-enriched uranium (enrichment… Read more »
Dear George, thanks for your reply. I chose my words very carefully here (and note I didn’t use the word “minimum”), which are confirmed by a historical review of the origin of the SQ values for HEU and plutonium. Stay tuned for more on this. Best, Ed Lyman
